questions.text: Which type of biopsy is most likely to provide tissue architecture but not the entire lesion?

answerOptions.text: A) Fine needle aspiration
B) Excisional biopsy
C) Incisional (wedge) biopsy
D) Cytology brush biopsy

questions.explanation: Incisional biopsy collects a sample of a lesion and allows evaluation of tissue architecture. It differs from excisional biopsy, which removes the entire lesion.

questions.text: Which technique is least recommended when removing a biopsy specimen for culture or cytologic evaluation?

answerOptions.text: A) Using a specimen retrieval pouch
B) Extracting through a wound protector
C) Electrocautery dissection of the lesion
D) Using atraumatic forceps for removal

questions.explanation: Electrocautery may sterilize or distort the specimen, compromising cultures or histopathology. Cold instruments or minimal-energy dissection is preferred.

questions.text: What is the correct gauge size typically used for laparoscopic core needle biopsy?

answerOptions.text: A) 20-gauge
B) 23–25 gauge
C) 14–18 gauge
D) 10–12 gauge

questions.explanation: Core biopsy needles commonly used laparoscopically fall in the 14–18 gauge range and provide a tissue cylinder for histologic evaluation.

questions.text: Which of the following is most appropriate when performing fine needle aspiration (FNA) under laparoscopic guidance?

answerOptions.text: A) Maintain suction while withdrawing the needle from the tissue
B) Use electrocautery to control bleeding before specimen collection
C) Release suction before removing the needle
D) Use a 10 mm trocar for needle placement

questions.explanation: Releasing the plunger (and thus the suction) before needle withdrawal prevents contamination of surrounding tissues and loss of aspirated cells.

questions.text: In which clinical situation is oophorectomy preferred over biopsy?

answerOptions.text: A) Dermoid cyst in a premenopausal patient
B) Corpus luteum cyst < 3 cm in premenopausal patient
C) Complex solid-cystic ovarian mass > 5 cm in a postmenopausal patient
D) Endometrioma in a 25-year-old

questions.explanation: In this scenario malignancy is potentially suspected; therefore, oophorectomy is preferred to avoid rupture, spillage, and seeding during biopsy.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a general principle of safe laparoscopic biopsy?

answerOptions.text: A) Avoid biopsy of fluid-filled vascular liver lesions
B) Use energy sources sparingly near vital structures
C) Always remove specimens with cautery to prevent bleeding
D) Place the specimen in a retrieval sac for extraction if large

questions.explanation: Energy sources should be avoided during removal as they can damage the specimen. Hemostasis should be performed after specimen retrieval.

questions.text: Which instrument is designed specifically for laparoscopic tissue biopsy?

answerOptions.text: A) Maryland dissector
B) Carter-Thomason device
C) Biopsy forceps
D) Endoscopic shears

questions.explanation: Laparoscopic biopsy forceps are designed with hollowed jaws and cutting rims to obtain clean tissue samples without crushing.

questions.text: What is the preferred hemostatic method after liver biopsy?

answerOptions.text: A) Ice lavage
B) Direct pressure and hemostatic agents
C) Routine suturing
D) Compression with ultrasonic probe

questions.explanation: Hemostasis after liver biopsy is typically achieved with direct pressure and hemostatic agents; suturing is not routinely required.

questions.text: Which site is not typically associated with laparoscopic lymph node biopsy?

answerOptions.text: A) Pelvic lymph nodes along the external iliac vein
B) Retroperitoneal lymph nodes along the aorta and common iliac vessels
C) Porta hepatis lymph nodes along the hepatoduodenal ligament
D) Mesenteric root lymph nodes

questions.explanation: Common laparoscopic lymph node biopsy sites include pelvic, retroperitoneal, and porta hepatis nodes. The mesenteric root is more difficult to access and higher risk, so it is not a typical target.

questions.text: Why should peritoneal washings be performed early in a cancer staging laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: A) To avoid contaminating the sample with blood
B) Because saline must dwell in the cavity for >30 minutes
C) To help with trocar placement planning
D) Because cytology equipment is only available early

questions.explanation: Early collection of peritoneal washings avoids blood and manipulation artifacts, ensuring a cleaner specimen for cytology or culture.

questions.text: How much fluid is typically used for peritoneal washings in staging procedures?

answerOptions.text: A) 20 cc
B) 50 cc
C) 100 cc
D) 500 cc

questions.explanation: Approximately 100 cc of normal saline is instilled and later aspirated for peritoneal washings in staging laparoscopies.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the use of core needle biopsy in the liver?

answerOptions.text: A) Preferred for cystic lesions
B) Should be avoided in cirrhosis
C) Commonly used for parenchymal disease and solid masses
D) Rarely used due to bleeding risk

questions.explanation: Core needle biopsy is frequently used for diagnosing cirrhosis or solid liver lesions. Caution is advised near vascular lesions like hemangiomas.

questions.text: Which biopsy method provides cytologic information only, not tissue architecture?

answerOptions.text: A) Incisional biopsy
B) Fine needle aspiration
C) Wedge biopsy
D) Core needle biopsy

questions.explanation: Fine needle aspiration yields cells for cytologic assessment but does not preserve tissue architecture.

questions.text: Why is care needed when biopsying hollow viscus surface lesions?

answerOptions.text: A) Risk of foreign body retention
B) Risk of postoperative diarrhea
C) Risk of tissue overgrowth
D) Risk of leakage or perforation

questions.explanation: Biopsying hollow organs can create defects; surgeons should consider reapproximating tissue to avoid leakage or perforation.

questions.text: When removing a larger biopsy specimen, which extraction method is preferred?

answerOptions.text: A) Through an EndoCatch bag via port
B) Using suction and electrocautery
C) Through the main suction cannister
D) Grasped directly and pulled through fascia

questions.explanation: Large specimens should be placed in a retrieval pouch to prevent tissue seeding or contamination of the port site during removal.

questions.text: Which of the following lesions should generally not be biopsied due to high bleeding risk?

answerOptions.text: A) Flat liver lesion
B) Ovarian dermoid
C) Hepatic hemangioma
D) Superficial peritoneal implant

questions.explanation: Hepatic hemangiomas are vascular lesions prone to significant bleeding if biopsied and are typically diagnosed radiographically instead.

questions.text: What is the primary reason for using ultrasound guidance during laparoscopic biopsy?

answerOptions.text: A) It replaces specimen handling
B) It improves surface tissue architecture
C) It helps localize deep or nonvisible lesions
D) It avoids the need for anesthesia

questions.explanation: Intraoperative ultrasound allows accurate targeting of lesions not visible on the peritoneal surface and reduces the risk of injury to adjacent structures.

questions.text: Which of the following techniques minimizes risk to vital structures during retroperitoneal biopsy?

answerOptions.text: A) Use of monopolar electrocautery adjacent to vessels
B) Use of suture ligature or Endoloop on nodal blood supply
C) Insertion of Veress needle without tenting abdominal walls
D) Sharp dissection with Metzenbaum scissors only

questions.explanation: When dissecting near critical structures (ureters, nerves, vessels), suture ligation is safer than thermal energy.

questions.text: What is the recommended technique to avoid specimen loss when collecting peritoneal washings?

answerOptions.text: A) Suction into the main canister directly
B) Allow saline to dwell for 15 minutes before suction
C) Use a trap between suction tubing and canister
D) Mix aspirate with formalin immediately

questions.explanation: A fluid trap ensures the sample is collected before reaching the main canister, preserving the specimen for cytology or culture.

questions.text: In a complex cystic ovarian lesion where complete cystectomy may be difficult, what is the most appropriate next step?

answerOptions.text: A) Perform cyst wall fenestration and aspirate fluid
B) Leave the lesion and re-image in 6 months
C) Convert to laparotomy for full removal
D) Refer to a specialist with expertise in complex laparoscopic cystectomy

questions.explanation: Complete removal of complex cysts may benefit the patient but requires advanced skill. Referral ensures appropriate care without compromising safety.
